title: NOC 94201 – Electronics assemblers, fabricators, inspectors and testers | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s electronics manufacturing sector is a high-growth industry, driven by the expansion of "Smart City" infrastructure, medical device innovation, and the domestic production of specialized telecommunications hardware. Electronics Assemblers, Fabricators, Inspectors, and Testers (NOC 94201) are the precision-focused technicians who build and verify the circuit boards and electronic components that power modern life. As Canada pushes for "Technological Sovereignty," international workers with experience in Surface Mount Technology (SMT) and automated optical inspection are in high demand, particularly in Ontario’s "Silicon Valley North."

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group942 – Assemblers and inspectors in manufacturing

duties: Precision Assembly: Solder, bolt, screw, or clip electronic components such as transistors, capacitors, and integrated circuits onto printed circuit boards.
Machine Operation: Operating automated assembly equipment, including SMT pick-and-place machines and wave-soldering lines.
Micro-Fabrication: Using microscopes and specialized hand tools to assemble miniature components or repair high-density circuit boards.
Testing & Calibration: Using digital multimeters, oscilloscopes, and automated test equipment (ATE) to verify the functionality of finished products.
2026 Update: Utilizing Augmented Reality (AR) headsets to follow complex 3D wiring schematics for high-reliability aerospace components.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is the standard requirement.
Training: On-the-job training is provided; however, a College Certificate in Electronics Manufacturing is highly preferred for 2026 technical roles.
Manual Dexterity: Exceptional hand-eye coordination and the ability to work with very small components under magnification.
Certification: IPC-A-610 (Acceptability of Electronic Assemblies) certification is a massive asset and often required for high-reliability manufacturing.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) expects a "Balanced" market. While basic assembly is increasingly robotic, the need for skilled Inspectors and Testers to manage these automated systems is projected to grow. Over 4,000 openings are expected due to the expansion of 5G infrastructure and green-tech electronics.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is characterized by a "High Skill Demand" as facilities transition to more complex micro-electronics:
Ontario (Ottawa & Waterloo): Very Good prospects. Ottawa’s telecommunications cluster and Waterloo’s tech hub are actively recruiting assemblers for specialized hardware and prototype development.
Quebec (Montreal): Strong demand. Driven by the aerospace and medical technology sectors, which require high-precision electronic assembly for flight controls and diagnostic tools.
British Columbia: Good prospects. Focused on specialized electronics for the green energy and marine technology sectors in the Lower Mainland.

National Trend: Approximately 18,500 people work in this occupation. While basic consumer electronics assembly has largely automated, the demand for high-reliability (medical/aerospace) assembly remains a top priority.
Why Work in Canada as an Electronics Assembler?
High-Tech Environment: Work with 2026-grade technologies, including robotic soldering arms, 3D Solder Paste Inspection (SPI), and clean-room environments.
Strategic Industry Growth: Be part of Canada’s move toward domestic semiconductor and advanced sensor manufacturing.
Path to PR: Frequently prioritized in Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) streams and eligible for the 2026 TR to PR transition programs as an "Essential Tech-Manufacturing" role.
Stable, Climate-Controlled Work: Unlike many manufacturing roles, electronics assembly is typically performed in clean, temperature-controlled, and ergonomic environments.
pathways: TR to PR Pathway (April 2026): Electronics assemblers are classified as "Essential Manufacturing Workers," making them high-priority candidates for this 33,000-person transition quota.
Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): This NOC is frequently eligible for provincial nomination for those with a valid job offer in Ontario’s tech corridor.
BC PNP Tech: While usually for TEER 0-3, highly skilled electronics testers (TEER 4) with IPC certification are often recruited through specialized regional pilots.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for technical manufacturing workers to obtain Permanent Residency with a provincial job offer.
